finding_results.notes: Repurposed the post-cereal half of the meeting so the first late beat imposes a concrete condition on Doug's apartment, sharpens his objection, and preserves the must-land 'That's the point, Doug' turn. The second late beat now sets the two-day Columbus content test, release requirements, legal limits, and schedule pressure, with follow-through questions that make the crew plan feel operational rather than sloganized.
finding_results.summary: The late Netflix meeting now escalates through access cost and production consequence instead of repeating that Doug's ordinary life is inherently compelling.

finding_results.evidence: Maya now introduces friction at chapters/chapter_06.md:69 ('We had two masculinity-conversation projects in development last year. Neither one got past testing.') and a budget/timeline concern at chapters/chapter_06.md:139 ('If we're shooting in Columbus, that budget's coming out of development. Different approval chain.') with a follow-up push at chapters/chapter_06.md:143 ('Just — I need to know before Thursday if we're —'). The other Brendan introduces a production concern at chapters/chapter_06.md:159 ('Is the building union?') and an access-logistics question at chapters/chapter_06.md:201 (elevator inquiry). Maya's lines are factual, flat, and operationally motivated — sortable from Charlie's enthusiasm and Layla's hype register by syntax and objective alone.
finding_results.notes: Four revisions across the meeting scene replace the autocomplete/approval-noise pattern. (1) The dad-core phrase-completion exchange (snapshot:67-73) is replaced: Charlie states the masculinity-conversation frame himself; Maya pushes back with a development-history fact; Charlie dismisses with an incomplete reframe ('Those were constructed. This is — we're not constructing anything here.'). (2) The anti-aspiration phrase-completion exchange (snapshot:141-143) is replaced: Maya introduces a budget-approval-chain concern and a Thursday deadline; Charlie overrides with 'Thursday is Thursday.' Both Maya exchanges give her a distinct motive (making production logistics work) and distinct syntax (short, declarative, practical) that separates her from Charlie's visionary momentum. (3) The other Brendan's pure-nodding beat (snapshot:159) is replaced with a specific union-status question addressed to no one, showing he has a real production concern even if the room ignores it. (4) The meeting summary (snapshot:197) now includes Brendan asking about the building's elevator before defaulting to his 'love that' habit, preserving the original counting joke while showing he contributed one real logistical question. Charlie's contraction level (high) and speech patterns (reframes, pivots, abandoned sentences) are maintained throughout. No example lines from the style bible appear in the chapter.

pass_summaries.summary: Revised the late-meeting section of chapter 6 so the scene still lands Doug being processed into content, but now does it through apartment access rules, workplace-clearance pressure, and a specific Columbus crew plan.

Revised four dialogue passages in the Netflix meeting scene to fix idiolect separation and autocomplete/approval-noise patterns. Maya's two phrase-completion exchanges were replaced with operational friction: she flags that the masculinity-conversation frame failed twice in development and raises a budget-approval-chain concern with a Thursday deadline. The other Brendan's pure nodding was replaced with a union-status question and an elevator-access inquiry. All support-player lines now introduce motives, concerns, or practical information that Charlie did not already state, while preserving Charlie's on-model enthusiasm, Doug's literal responses, and Layla's hype register. A cold reader can now sort Charlie, Maya, and Layla by syntax and objective in the target passages.

Addressed the prose/copyedit packet by varying the chapter's key descriptive beats toward cleaner, more concrete Doug-bound observation without softening the chapter's discomfort or continuity signals.
